title: practical english usage - Part 342
content:
Only you could do a thing like that.

Only normally comes before a subject that it refers to.

1  referring       to  the   subject a sentence.

Only can be used as a focusing adverbial (►   199). It can refer to different parts of 545 only:    focusing      adverbial vocabulary •  549  opposite, facing and in front of The man sitting across from me was smoking a pipe. (AmE) not She stood at the other side of the table facing me. (     .. .-in front of-me?) not There's a garage opposite my house, (     .. . in front of my house.) is usually expressed with opposite or facing (AmE  also across from).

We   do not use in front of to mean ‘ across a road/river/room, etc from’ . This idea 1 'across     a  road/room,       etc   from':   opposite/facing 549 opposite,         facing       and   in   front      of ►     42H.

For opposite and contrary, The woman she was looking for was in the house directly opposite.

noi                                                                               mr,) (    . . . the opposite man teas staring at I noticed that the man opposite was staring at me.

‘ facing a person or place that has already been mentioned'.

We   put opposite after the noun when it means ‘ facing the speaker or listener' or His brother was fighting on the opposite side.

I've got exactly the opposite opinion to yours.

She went off in the opposite direction.

I think the picture would look better on the opposite wall.

pair of things that naturally face or contrast with each other.

We   put the adjective opposite before a noun when we  are talking about one of a 548  opposite         (adjective):     position (More natural than I have the possibility to go to Denmark...) There's a possibility of my going to Denmark next year. (= 1 may go . . .) a possibility of something happening.

Possibility is not often used in this structure, ɪt is more normal to say that there is I have the opportunity to go to Denmark next year. (= I can go .. .) We   often say that somebody has the opportunity to do / of doing something.

547 oppo     rtunity        a nd   poss    ibility ►     422.

For closed and shut, not Who left the taps turned on? (     Who left the taps open?) not Could you turn/switch the radio on? (     .. .-open the-radie?) How do you unfasten this belt?

I can't untie/undo this shoelace, (     l-ean'hopen this shoelace.) switches or taps.

Note that open is not the normal word to refer to the fastenings of clothes, or to 2 when     open    is  not   used She opened her eyes and sat up. The safe was opened with dynamite.

about the action of opening.

Opened is used as the past tense and past participle of the verb open, to talk Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  550  other(s) and another Those cakes are wonderful. Could I have another (one)?

what has come before.

Another can be used without a noun, or with one, if the meaning is clear from not Could I have another piece of bread? (     ... an other piece of bread.) singular countable nouns.

Another (spelt as one word) can mean 'an additional, extra'. It is used with 3 'additional,       extra' For one another, ►     179.

You look different with a beard. (     You look-other. . .) not I'd prefer a completely different colour. (     ... a completely other colour.) So it cannot normally have an adverb before it, or be used after a linking verb.

Other is a determiner or pronoun; it is not used exactly like an adjective.

2 not   used    like   an   adjective He never thinks of others.  Jake's arrived -I must tell the others.

mean (the) other people.

mentioned before. An exception is the common plural use of (the) others to Normally, other(s) is only used alone if it refers to a noun that has been These are too small. Have you got any others?

I've got one lot of photos. Where are the others?

But used alone, without a noun, it can have a plural form.

Where are the other photos? (     ... the others photos?) When  other is used with a noun it has no plural form.

1 other      and   others 550 other(s)      and   another For the difference between before and in front of ►    397.
